The 2.5mg dose is used to initiate therapy in elderly patients, those with renal impairment, or patients prone to first-dose hypotension. The landmark HOPE trial demonstrated that Ramipril significantly reduces cardiovascular death, MI, and stroke in high-risk patients. Titrated to 5–10mg maintenance dose.

Synthetic mineralocorticoid that binds aldosterone receptors in the renal distal tubule, promoting sodium and water reabsorption to expand intravascular volume and raise blood pressure. First-line pharmacological treatment for orthostatic (low) blood pressure and primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). Effective for chronic hypotension with normal or elevated Na/K ratio. Requires monitoring for peripheral edema, hypokalemia, hypertension, and cardiac decompensation with long-term use.

Thiazide-like diuretic with antihypertensive properties mediated by both mild diuresis and direct vascular smooth muscle relaxation. At the 1.5mg sustained-release dose, the vasodilatory mechanism predominates over diuresis, reducing peripheral vascular resistance with fewer metabolic side effects than conventional thiazides. Metabolically neutral — does not adversely affect lipid profile or glucose tolerance. Preferred over HCTZ in elderly, diabetic, and dyslipidemic hypertensive patients.

Fixed-dose combination of Amlodipine 5mg (calcium channel blocker) and Atorvastatin 20mg (moderate-intensity statin). Addresses two major cardiovascular risk factors simultaneously: high blood pressure and elevated LDL cholesterol. Amlodipine dilates peripheral arteries to reduce blood pressure; Atorvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, lowering LDL by 38–46%. Reduces pill burden and improves treatment adherence in patients with hypertension and dyslipidemia.

Moderate-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 35–42% through HMG-CoA reductase inhibition. Used for management of hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia in moderate cardiovascular risk patients. Evening dosing aligns with peak nocturnal cholesterol synthesis. FDA 2011 warning advises against the 80mg dose due to unacceptable myopathy risk; 20mg remains a clinically safe and effective dose. Monitor for myalgia and periodic liver enzyme assessment.

Low-dose synthetic thyroxine (T4) hormone replacement for hypothyroidism. Starting dose of 25mcg is indicated in elderly patients (>65 years), those with cardiac disease, or patients with mild hypothyroidism to minimize the risk of cardiac arrhythmia or angina from rapid thyroid hormone increase. Levothyroxine is peripherally converted to active triiodothyronine (T3), regulating basal metabolic rate, cardiovascular function, and growth. Serum TSH must be measured every 6–8 weeks; dose titrated in 12.5–25mcg increments.

Combination of ACE inhibitor Lisinopril 20mg and high-intensity statin Rosuvastatin 20mg for patients with hypertension and high cardiovascular risk. Lisinopril 20mg provides robust blood pressure reduction with additional nephroprotection, particularly beneficial in diabetic or CKD patients. Rosuvastatin 20mg reduces LDL by 52–55%. This combination targets both hypertension and hyperlipidemia to maximally reduce risk of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular mortality in high-risk patients.

Combination of high-intensity statin Rosuvastatin 20mg and fibrate Fenofibrate 145mg for mixed dyslipidemia with both elevated LDL and high triglycerides. Rosuvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to reduce LDL by 52–55%. Fenofibrate activates PPAR-alpha transcription factor, stimulating fatty acid oxidation and reducing hepatic triglyceride synthesis while raising HDL cholesterol by 10–20%. Fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate with statins due to lower pharmacokinetic interaction and myopathy risk compared to gemfibrozil.

Cholesterol absorption inhibitor that selectively blocks the NPC1L1 transporter in the intestinal brush border, reducing dietary and biliary cholesterol absorption by approximately 55%. Reduces LDL cholesterol by 18–25% as monotherapy. Used in patients with high cholesterol who are statin-intolerant, or as adjunct therapy to statins when LDL targets are not met. Minimal systemic absorption ensures a favorable safety profile. Does not affect absorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Once-daily dosing at any time.

Isotonic oral sodium chloride solution providing physiological saline (0.9% NaCl equivalent) for fluid and electrolyte replacement in mild-to-moderate dehydration, hypovolemia, and hypotension. Increases intravascular sodium and water content to raise blood pressure and restore circulating volume. Used in low BP patients with hypovolemia from inadequate oral intake or mild sodium depletion. Safe across all age groups. First-line non-pharmacological intervention for mild hypotension before escalating to vasopressors.

Standard synthetic T4 replacement for hypothyroidism in younger adults with moderate thyroid hormone deficiency. Restores euthyroid state by replacing deficient thyroxine, normalizing TSH, and resolving symptoms of fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and cognitive slowing. Taken on an empty stomach 30–60 minutes before breakfast for optimal and consistent absorption. Avoid co-administration with calcium, iron, or PPIs which reduce absorption. Annual TSH monitoring required once stable dose is achieved.

Extended-release formulation of cardioselective beta-1 adrenergic blocker Metoprolol. Reduces heart rate, myocardial contractility, and cardiac output to lower blood pressure. The succinate salt provides sustained 24-hour release, maintaining consistent beta-blockade and improving adherence. Second- or third-line antihypertensive per JNC 8. First-line for concurrent heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), angina, and post-myocardial infarction. Monitor for bradycardia, fatigue, and bronchoconstriction in asthma patients.

Oral rehydration solution combined with Zinc 20mg for electrolyte correction and immune support. ORS provides sodium, potassium, glucose, and bicarbonate to restore fluid and electrolyte balance rapidly via glucose-sodium co-transport in the intestinal mucosa. Zinc 20mg enhances gut mucosal recovery, reduces intestinal permeability, shortens diarrheal illness duration, and boosts innate immune response. WHO-recommended combination for treatment of dehydration and electrolyte imbalance associated with elevated Na/K ratio.

Low-dose antiplatelet agent that irreversibly acetylates and inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis and reducing platelet aggregation. Prescribed at 100mg for secondary prevention of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). Not recommended for primary prevention in low-to-moderate risk individuals per current ACC/AHA 2019 guidelines.

Synthetic mineralocorticoid that binds aldosterone receptors in the renal distal tubule, promoting sodium and water reabsorption to expand intravascular volume and raise blood pressure. First-line pharmacological treatment for orthostatic (low) blood pressure and primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). Effective for chronic hypotension with normal or elevated Na/K ratio. Requires monitoring for peripheral edema, hypokalemia, hypertension, and cardiac decompensation with long-term use.

A fixed-dose combination of Amlodipine 10mg (calcium channel blocker) and Atorvastatin 40mg (high-intensity statin). Amlodipine blocks vascular L-type calcium channels, causing arterial vasodilation and reducing peripheral resistance to lower high blood pressure. Atorvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, reducing LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Co-prescribed for patients with concurrent hypertension and high cholesterol to simplify therapy and improve cardiovascular outcomes.

Fixed-dose combination of Amlodipine 5mg (calcium channel blocker) and Atorvastatin 20mg (moderate-intensity statin). Addresses two major cardiovascular risk factors simultaneously: high blood pressure and elevated LDL cholesterol. Amlodipine dilates peripheral arteries to reduce blood pressure; Atorvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, lowering LDL by 38–46%. Reduces pill burden and improves treatment adherence in patients with hypertension and dyslipidemia.

A low starting dose of Amlodipine, a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker. Blocks L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation and lowering blood pressure. The 2.5mg dose is used to initiate therapy in elderly, frail, or sensitive patients with mild hypertension to minimize the risk of hypotension and peripheral edema. Titrated upward to 5mg or 10mg based on blood pressure response and tolerability.

Combination of beta-1 selective blocker Metoprolol 50mg and low-intensity statin Atorvastatin 10mg. Metoprolol reduces heart rate and cardiac output to lower blood pressure, and is beneficial in patients with concurrent angina or heart failure. Atorvastatin 10mg provides mild LDL reduction (30–37%). Co-prescribed for patients with high BP and mild dyslipidemia, particularly post-MI patients who require both heart rate control and lipid lowering. Beta-blockers are second-line antihypertensives per JNC 8.

Combination of ACE inhibitor Ramipril 5mg and low-intensity statin Atorvastatin 10mg for mild hypertension with mild dyslipidemia. Ramipril at 5mg provides effective blood pressure control with nephroprotective and cardioprotective benefits supported by HOPE trial evidence. Atorvastatin 10mg lowers LDL by 30–37%. Co-prescribed for patients with diabetes or early CKD who have both mild high BP and mild cholesterol elevation, providing additive cardiovascular risk reduction at tolerable doses.

Synthetic mineralocorticoid that binds aldosterone receptors in the renal distal tubule, promoting sodium and water reabsorption to expand intravascular volume and raise blood pressure. First-line pharmacological treatment for orthostatic (low) blood pressure and primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). Effective for chronic hypotension with normal or elevated Na/K ratio. Requires monitoring for peripheral edema, hypokalemia, hypertension, and cardiac decompensation with long-term use.

Low starting dose of ACE inhibitor Ramipril for hypertension, heart failure, or renal protection in high-risk patients. Inhibits ACE to block angiotensin II formation, reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sodium retention. The 2.5mg dose is used to initiate therapy in elderly patients, those with renal impairment, or patients prone to first-dose hypotension. The landmark HOPE trial demonstrated that Ramipril significantly reduces cardiovascular death, MI, and stroke in high-risk patients. Titrated to 5–10mg maintenance dose.

High-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to markedly reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and increase LDL receptor expression. Recommended for patients with established ASCVD, familial hypercholesterolemia, or very high cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA and NICE guidelines. Standard of care for secondary cardiovascular prevention. Monitor for myalgia, elevated creatine kinase, and hepatic transaminases at initiation.

High-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to markedly reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and increase LDL receptor expression. Recommended for patients with established ASCVD, familial hypercholesterolemia, or very high cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA and NICE guidelines. Standard of care for secondary cardiovascular prevention. Monitor for myalgia, elevated creatine kinase, and hepatic transaminases at initiation.

Combined oral rehydration solution with supplementary sodium chloride tablets (600mg = 10.3 mEq sodium) for management of significant sodium depletion and hypotension. Standard ORS alone may be insufficient in patients with marked hyponatremia or hypotension from sodium-wasting conditions. Additional NaCl 600mg raises intravascular sodium concentration to restore blood pressure and correct serum sodium deficit. Used before escalation to intravenous saline therapy. Monitor serum sodium levels to avoid overly rapid correction.

Oral rehydration solution combined with Zinc 20mg for electrolyte correction and immune support. ORS provides sodium, potassium, glucose, and bicarbonate to restore fluid and electrolyte balance rapidly via glucose-sodium co-transport in the intestinal mucosa. Zinc 20mg enhances gut mucosal recovery, reduces intestinal permeability, shortens diarrheal illness duration, and boosts innate immune response. WHO-recommended combination for treatment of dehydration and electrolyte imbalance associated with elevated Na/K ratio.

High-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to markedly reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and increase LDL receptor expression. Recommended for patients with established ASCVD, familial hypercholesterolemia, or very high cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA and NICE guidelines. Standard of care for secondary cardiovascular prevention. Monitor for myalgia, elevated creatine kinase, and hepatic transaminases at initiation.

WHO-standard oral rehydration solution providing sodium 75 mEq/L, potassium 20 mEq/L, chloride 65 mEq/L, citrate 10 mEq/L, and glucose 75 mmol/L in a reduced-osmolarity formulation. Restores fluid and electrolyte balance via intestinal glucose-sodium co-transport, which drives passive water absorption. First-line WHO-recommended treatment for mild-to-moderate dehydration, hypovolemia, and hypotension due to fluid loss. Reduces the need for intravenous rehydration. Safe for all age groups including infants and elderly patients.

Proton pump inhibitor (PPI) that irreversibly inhibits the H+/K+-ATPase enzyme (proton pump) in gastric parietal cells, reducing gastric acid secretion by over 90%. Prescribed for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), peptic ulcer disease, Helicobacter pylori eradication (as part of triple therapy), and gastroprotection in patients on chronic NSAIDs or antiplatelet therapy such as aspirin. Often co-prescribed with cardiovascular medications to prevent GI bleeding. Taken 30 minutes before a meal for maximum efficacy.

ACE inhibitor that inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme, blocking conversion of angiotensin I to the potent vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Reduces peripheral vascular resistance, aldosterone secretion, and sodium retention, lowering blood pressure. At 10mg, used for established hypertension and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Dry cough occurs in 5–20% of patients due to bradykinin accumulation. Contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis, pregnancy, and history of ACE inhibitor-associated angioedema.

Low-dose synthetic thyroxine (T4) hormone replacement for hypothyroidism. Starting dose of 25mcg is indicated in elderly patients (>65 years), those with cardiac disease, or patients with mild hypothyroidism to minimize the risk of cardiac arrhythmia or angina from rapid thyroid hormone increase. Levothyroxine is peripherally converted to active triiodothyronine (T3), regulating basal metabolic rate, cardiovascular function, and growth. Serum TSH must be measured every 6–8 weeks; dose titrated in 12.5–25mcg increments.

Very high-intensity statin for maximum LDL reduction of 55–65% in very high-risk patients. Indicated for familial hypercholesterolemia, established ASCVD with suboptimal LDL control despite lower statin doses, or post-ACS patients requiring aggressive secondary prevention. Recommended as monotherapy or in combination with Ezetimibe when LDL targets (<55 mg/dL for very high-risk) cannot be met with lower doses. Monitor liver enzymes and creatine kinase at initiation. Dose capped at 20mg in Asian populations.

Low-dose antiplatelet used for long-term cardiovascular risk reduction. Irreversibly inhibits platelet COX-1, reducing thromboxane A2 production and arterial thrombosis risk. The 75mg dose delivers equivalent antiplatelet effect to 100mg with marginally lower gastrointestinal bleeding risk. Used in elderly patients and those with established cardiovascular disease for secondary prevention. Often co-prescribed with Omeprazole to reduce GI complications.

Synthetic mineralocorticoid that binds aldosterone receptors in the renal distal tubule, promoting sodium and water reabsorption to expand intravascular volume and raise blood pressure. First-line pharmacological treatment for orthostatic (low) blood pressure and primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). Effective for chronic hypotension with normal or elevated Na/K ratio. Requires monitoring for peripheral edema, hypokalemia, hypertension, and cardiac decompensation with long-term use.

Standard synthetic T4 replacement for hypothyroidism in younger adults with moderate thyroid hormone deficiency. Restores euthyroid state by replacing deficient thyroxine, normalizing TSH, and resolving symptoms of fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and cognitive slowing. Taken on an empty stomach 30–60 minutes before breakfast for optimal and consistent absorption. Avoid co-administration with calcium, iron, or PPIs which reduce absorption. Annual TSH monitoring required once stable dose is achieved.

Combination of high-intensity statin Rosuvastatin 20mg and fibrate Fenofibrate 145mg for mixed dyslipidemia with both elevated LDL and high triglycerides. Rosuvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to reduce LDL by 52–55%. Fenofibrate activates PPAR-alpha transcription factor, stimulating fatty acid oxidation and reducing hepatic triglyceride synthesis while raising HDL cholesterol by 10–20%. Fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate with statins due to lower pharmacokinetic interaction and myopathy risk compared to gemfibrozil.

Moderate-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 45–50% with high hepatic selectivity and low systemic exposure, minimizing the risk of myopathy. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and upregulate LDL receptors. Also raises HDL cholesterol by 8–10%. Prescribed for moderate cardiovascular risk patients with high cholesterol. Once-daily dosing at any time of day due to long 19-hour half-life. Lower myopathy risk than equivalent doses of simvastatin or lovastatin.

Analgesic and antipyretic that inhibits central prostaglandin synthesis via COX enzyme modulation in the CNS, reducing pain signal transmission and fever. Standard dose of 500mg for mild-to-moderate pain and fever management. Has a favorable cardiovascular safety profile — unlike NSAIDs, does not affect platelet function, blood pressure, or renal prostaglandin synthesis. Preferred analgesic for patients on antihypertensive, antiplatelet, or anticoagulant therapy. Hepatotoxic in overdose — maximum safe dose is 4g/day in healthy adults and 2g/day in liver disease or alcohol use.

High-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to markedly reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and increase LDL receptor expression. Recommended for patients with established ASCVD, familial hypercholesterolemia, or very high cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA and NICE guidelines. Standard of care for secondary cardiovascular prevention. Monitor for myalgia, elevated creatine kinase, and hepatic transaminases at initiation.

Low-dose antiplatelet agent that irreversibly acetylates and inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis and reducing platelet aggregation. Prescribed at 100mg for secondary prevention of myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). Not recommended for primary prevention in low-to-moderate risk individuals per current ACC/AHA 2019 guidelines.

Bile acid sequestrant (resin) that binds bile acids in the intestinal lumen, preventing enterohepatic recirculation. This forces the liver to convert more cholesterol into new bile acids, reducing hepatic and serum LDL cholesterol by 15–30%. Used for hypercholesterolemia in patients who are statin-intolerant or require additional LDL lowering. Not systemically absorbed — safe in pregnancy and children. May interfere with absorption of co-administered drugs; administer other drugs 1 hour before or 4–6 hours after Cholestyramine.

Combination of ACE inhibitor Lisinopril 10mg and moderate-intensity statin Atorvastatin 20mg for simultaneous management of hypertension and dyslipidemia. Lisinopril inhibits ACE to block angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction, reducing blood pressure and providing renal protection in diabetic patients. Atorvastatin reduces LDL cholesterol by 38–46%. Together, they address two major independent cardiovascular risk factors, significantly reducing 10-year cardiovascular event risk in patients with both high BP and high cholesterol.

Low-dose synthetic thyroxine (T4) hormone replacement for hypothyroidism. Starting dose of 25mcg is indicated in elderly patients (>65 years), those with cardiac disease, or patients with mild hypothyroidism to minimize the risk of cardiac arrhythmia or angina from rapid thyroid hormone increase. Levothyroxine is peripherally converted to active triiodothyronine (T3), regulating basal metabolic rate, cardiovascular function, and growth. Serum TSH must be measured every 6–8 weeks; dose titrated in 12.5–25mcg increments.

First-line oral antidiabetic biguanide for type 2 diabetes mellitus. Primarily inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and reduces hepatic glucose output; also improves peripheral insulin sensitivity and reduces intestinal glucose absorption. Does not cause hypoglycemia or weight gain. Starting dose of 500mg with meals minimizes GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) during initiation. UKPDS trial demonstrated significant reduction in diabetes-related complications and cardiovascular mortality. Contraindicated in renal failure (eGFR <30 mL/min) due to lactic acidosis risk.

Moderate-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 38–46% through HMG-CoA reductase inhibition. First-line lipid-lowering therapy per ACC/AHA guidelines for patients with elevated cardiovascular risk and high cholesterol. Also provides plaque-stabilizing and anti-inflammatory effects that are independent of lipid lowering (pleiotropic effects). Once-daily dosing at any time. Liver function should be checked if symptoms of hepatotoxicity develop.

Low-intensity statin for patients with mild hyperlipidemia or those initiating statin therapy who are statin-sensitive. Inhibits hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, reducing LDL cholesterol by approximately 30–37%. Suitable for younger patients or those at lower cardiovascular risk. Best taken in the evening to align with peak hepatic cholesterol synthesis. Well-tolerated at this dose with minimal risk of myopathy.

Isotonic oral sodium chloride solution providing physiological saline (0.9% NaCl equivalent) for fluid and electrolyte replacement in mild-to-moderate dehydration, hypovolemia, and hypotension. Increases intravascular sodium and water content to raise blood pressure and restore circulating volume. Used in low BP patients with hypovolemia from inadequate oral intake or mild sodium depletion. Safe across all age groups. First-line non-pharmacological intervention for mild hypotension before escalating to vasopressors.

Combination of ARB Losartan 50mg and moderate-intensity statin Rosuvastatin 10mg for patients with both hypertension and elevated cholesterol. Losartan lowers blood pressure via selective AT1 receptor blockade without affecting bradykinin. Rosuvastatin 10mg reduces LDL by 45–50% and raises HDL by 8–10%. This combination reduces pill burden and addresses two independent cardiovascular risk factors, improving long-term adherence and cardiovascular outcomes.

Marine-derived polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA + DHA) at 1000mg for dyslipidemia management, particularly elevated triglycerides. Reduces hepatic VLDL triglyceride synthesis, increases lipoprotein lipase activity for triglyceride clearance, and modestly lowers LDL. Prescribed as first-line non-statin therapy for mild dyslipidemia in younger adults or as adjunct to statins. Also provides anti-inflammatory, anti-arrhythmic, and cardioprotective benefits. Well-tolerated; main side effect is fishy aftertaste.

Thiazide diuretic and first-line antihypertensive per JNC 8 guidelines. Inhibits the Na-Cl cotransporter (NCC) in the renal distal convoluted tubule, reducing sodium and water reabsorption to decrease intravascular volume and lower blood pressure. Effective in most adult patients with high BP, particularly when Na/K ratio is elevated. Preferred in Black patients and elderly with isolated systolic hypertension. Monitor for hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, and dyslipidemia with long-term use.

Potent loop diuretic that inhibits the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter (NKCC2) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, producing rapid and profound sodium, potassium, chloride, and water excretion. Used for resistant hypertension, hypertensive urgency, pulmonary edema, and fluid overload states. Indicated in elderly patients with high blood pressure and fluid retention. Strictly contraindicated in hypotension as it can precipitate cardiovascular collapse. Requires regular monitoring of serum electrolytes, particularly potassium, due to significant hypokalemia risk.

Oral potassium supplement (600mg tablet provides approximately 8 mEq elemental potassium) for correction of hypokalemia (low serum potassium). Prescribed when the Na/K ratio falls below 10, indicating potassium depletion relative to sodium. Potassium chloride is the preferred potassium salt per AAFP guidelines, particularly when hypokalemia accompanies metabolic alkalosis or diuretic-induced potassium loss. Contraindicated in hyperkalemia and severe renal failure (eGFR <30 mL/min). Serum potassium levels must be monitored every 2–4 hours during active repletion.

Angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) that selectively antagonizes AT1 receptors, preventing angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sodium retention to lower blood pressure. Standard 50mg dose for hypertension management. Preferred over ACE inhibitors in female patients as it does not cause dry cough. Also provides renoprotection in type 2 diabetic nephropathy (FDA-approved indication). Contraindicated in pregnancy. Must never be combined with ACE inhibitors per dual RAAS blockade safety data.

Fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate with statins due to lower pharmacokinetic interaction and myopathy risk compared to gemfibrozil.

Very high-intensity statin for maximum LDL reduction of 55–65% in very high-risk patients. Indicated for familial hypercholesterolemia, established ASCVD with suboptimal LDL control despite lower statin doses, or post-ACS patients requiring aggressive secondary prevention. Recommended as monotherapy or in combination with Ezetimibe when LDL targets (<55 mg/dL for very high-risk) cannot be met with lower doses. Monitor liver enzymes and creatine kinase at initiation. Dose capped at 20mg in Asian populations.

Cholesterol absorption inhibitor that selectively blocks the NPC1L1 transporter in the intestinal brush border, reducing dietary and biliary cholesterol absorption by approximately 55%. Reduces LDL cholesterol by 18–25% as monotherapy. Used in patients with high cholesterol who are statin-intolerant, or as adjunct therapy to statins when LDL targets are not met. Minimal systemic absorption ensures a favorable safety profile. Does not affect absorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Once-daily dosing at any time.

Oral calcium supplement containing 40% elemental calcium — the highest elemental calcium content of any calcium salt. Used to correct hypocalcemia, prevent osteoporosis, and supplement dietary calcium deficiency. Requires adequate gastric acid for optimal absorption; best taken with meals. Also used as a phosphate binder in chronic kidney disease. Adjunct therapy in patients on long-term corticosteroids or proton pump inhibitors that reduce calcium absorption.

Potassium-sparing diuretic and competitive aldosterone receptor antagonist that blocks aldosterone action in the renal distal tubule, reducing sodium retention without increasing urinary potassium loss. Used as third-line antihypertensive for resistant hypertension where first-line agents are insufficient, particularly when the Na/K ratio is elevated (18–25). Also indicated for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (RALES trial) and primary hyperaldosteronism. Contraindicated in hypotension, hyperkalemia, and severe renal impairment (eGFR <30 mL/min).

Combination of high-intensity statin Rosuvastatin 20mg and fibrate Fenofibrate 145mg for mixed dyslipidemia with both elevated LDL and high triglycerides. Rosuvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to reduce LDL by 52–55%. Fenofibrate activates PPAR-alpha transcription factor, stimulating fatty acid oxidation and reducing hepatic triglyceride synthesis while raising HDL cholesterol by 10–20%. Fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate with statins due to lower pharmacokinetic interaction and myopathy risk compared to gemfibrozil.

First-line oral antidiabetic biguanide for type 2 diabetes mellitus. Primarily inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and reduces hepatic glucose output; also improves peripheral insulin sensitivity and reduces intestinal glucose absorption. Does not cause hypoglycemia or weight gain. Starting dose of 500mg with meals minimizes GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) during initiation. UKPDS trial demonstrated significant reduction in diabetes-related complications and cardiovascular mortality. Contraindicated in renal failure (eGFR <30 mL/min) due to lactic acidosis risk.

Standard synthetic T4 replacement for hypothyroidism in younger adults with moderate thyroid hormone deficiency. Restores euthyroid state by replacing deficient thyroxine, normalizing TSH, and resolving symptoms of fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and cognitive slowing. Taken on an empty stomach 30–60 minutes before breakfast for optimal and consistent absorption. Avoid co-administration with calcium, iron, or PPIs which reduce absorption. Annual TSH monitoring required once stable dose is achieved.

Moderate-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 38–46% through HMG-CoA reductase inhibition. First-line lipid-lowering therapy per ACC/AHA guidelines for patients with elevated cardiovascular risk and high cholesterol. Also provides plaque-stabilizing and anti-inflammatory effects that are independent of lipid lowering (pleiotropic effects). Once-daily dosing at any time. Liver function should be checked if symptoms of hepatotoxicity develop.

A fixed-dose combination of Amlodipine 10mg (calcium channel blocker) and Atorvastatin 40mg (high-intensity statin). Amlodipine blocks vascular L-type calcium channels, causing arterial vasodilation and reducing peripheral resistance to lower high blood pressure. Atorvastatin inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, reducing LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Co-prescribed for patients with concurrent hypertension and high cholesterol to simplify therapy and improve cardiovascular outcomes.

Moderate-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 45–50% with high hepatic selectivity and low systemic exposure, minimizing the risk of myopathy. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and upregulate LDL receptors. Also raises HDL cholesterol by 8–10%. Prescribed for moderate cardiovascular risk patients with high cholesterol. Once-daily dosing at any time of day due to long 19-hour half-life. Lower myopathy risk than equivalent doses of simvastatin or lovastatin.

Standard synthetic T4 replacement for hypothyroidism in younger adults with moderate thyroid hormone deficiency. Restores euthyroid state by replacing deficient thyroxine, normalizing TSH, and resolving symptoms of fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and cognitive slowing. Taken on an empty stomach 30–60 minutes before breakfast for optimal and consistent absorption. Avoid co-administration with calcium, iron, or PPIs which reduce absorption. Annual TSH monitoring required once stable dose is achieved.

High-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 50–60%. Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase to markedly reduce hepatic cholesterol synthesis and increase LDL receptor expression. Recommended for patients with established ASCVD, familial hypercholesterolemia, or very high cardiovascular risk per ACC/AHA and NICE guidelines. Standard of care for secondary cardiovascular prevention. Monitor for myalgia, elevated creatine kinase, and hepatic transaminases at initiation.

Moderate-intensity statin that reduces LDL cholesterol by 38–46% through HMG-CoA reductase inhibition. First-line lipid-lowering therapy per ACC/AHA guidelines for patients with elevated cardiovascular risk and high cholesterol. Also provides plaque-stabilizing and anti-inflammatory effects that are independent of lipid lowering (pleiotropic effects). Once-daily dosing at any time. Liver function should be checked if symptoms of hepatotoxicity develop.

Combination of high-intensity statin Rosuvastatin 20mg and fibrate Fenofibrate 145mg for mixed dyslipidemia with both elevated LDL and high triglycerides. Fenofibrate activates PPAR-alpha transcription factor, stimulating fatty acid oxidation and reducing hepatic triglyceride synthesis while raising HDL cholesterol by 10–20%. Fenofibrate is the preferred fibrate with statins due to lower pharmacokinetic interaction and myopathy risk compared to gemfibrozil.

Fixed-dose combination of Ezetimibe 10mg (cholesterol absorption inhibitor) and Simvastatin 20mg (moderate statin). Dual lipid-lowering mechanism: Simvastatin inhibits hepatic HMG-CoA reductase to reduce cholesterol synthesis; Ezetimibe blocks intestinal NPC1L1 to reduce cholesterol absorption. Combined LDL reduction of 45–55%, superior to either agent alone. Prescribed for patients with high cholesterol requiring greater LDL lowering than statin monotherapy, or those with statin intolerance at higher doses.

Minimal systemic absorption ensures a favorable safety profile. Does not affect absorption of fat-soluble vitamins. Once-daily dosing at any time.

Marine-derived polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA + DHA) at 1000mg for dyslipidemia management, particularly elevated triglycerides. Reduces hepatic VLDL triglyceride synthesis, increases lipoprotein lipase activity for triglyceride clearance, and modestly lowers LDL. Prescribed as first-line non-statin therapy for mild dyslipidemia in younger adults or as adjunct to statins. Also provides anti-inflammatory, anti-arrhythmic, and cardioprotective benefits. Well-tolerated; main side effect is fishy aftertaste.

WHO-standard oral rehydration solution providing sodium 75 mEq/L, potassium 20 mEq/L, chloride 65 mEq/L, citrate 10 mEq/L, and glucose 75 mmol/L in a reduced-osmolarity formulation. Restores fluid and electrolyte balance via intestinal glucose-sodium co-transport, which drives passive water absorption. First-line WHO-recommended treatment for mild-to-moderate dehydration, hypovolemia, and hypotension due to fluid loss. Reduces the need for intravenous rehydration. Safe for all age groups including infants and elderly patients.
